title: Cosmic Microwave Background
puzzle: Why do astronomers call faint microwave light the afterglow of the universe?
insight: The cosmic microwave background is ancient light released when the universe cooled enough for atoms to form and light could finally travel freely.
key_ideas: It comes from about 380,000 years after the Big Bang.
Tiny temperature variations reveal early structure.
It helps test models of the universe.
mind_blower: When astronomers map the microwave background, they are looking at the oldest light humans can directly observe.
next_curiosity: If this afterglow captures the young universe, what does it reveal about how galaxies later formed?
narration_text: Why do astronomers call faint microwave light the afterglow of the universe? The cosmic microwave background is ancient light released when the universe cooled enough for atoms to form and light could finally travel freely. It comes from about 380,000 years after the Big Bang. Tiny temperature variations reveal early structure. It helps test models of the universe. When astronomers map the microwave background, they are looking at the oldest light humans can directly observe. If this afterglow captures the young universe, what does it reveal about how galaxies later formed?
